lookup_context: Converging runway operation
Projected flight paths will cross
Following aircraft is taking off behind a landing or departing aircraft on the converging runway
items.task: Choose when the required time interval must be applied.
items.instruction: Use the converging runway wake turbulence figures.
items.question_text: When must the controller apply the listed wake turbulence time interval for a following departure on a converging runway?

choices.text: Only when the following aircraft uses the same runway.
When projected flight paths will cross.
Only when the preceding aircraft is airborne before the following aircraft is cleared.
Whenever the runways are within 2,500 feet of each other.

items.explanation: For nonintersecting converging runway operations, the wake turbulence intervals apply when aircraft take off behind a landing or departing aircraft on a converging runway and the projected flight paths will cross.

lookup_context: Converging runway wake turbulence interval is required
Pilot asks to waive or shorten the time interval
Preceding aircraft requires wake turbulence separation
items.instruction: Apply the converging runway wake turbulence limitation.
items.question_text: May the controller approve the pilot's request to deviate from the required time interval?

choices.text: Yes, if both aircraft are visually separated.
Yes, if the following aircraft accepts responsibility for wake turbulence.
No, not when the preceding aircraft requires wake turbulence separation.
Yes, but only after issuing a traffic advisory.

items.explanation: Paragraph 3-9-9 states not to approve pilot requests to deviate from the required time interval if the preceding aircraft requires wake turbulence separation.

lookup_context: Aircraft is operating at and below FL 410
Magnetic course is 180 through 359 degrees
Use altitude assignment by flight direction
items.question_text: For an IFR aircraft at or below FL 410 on course 180 through 359 degrees magnetic, which altitude family applies?

choices.text: Odd cardinal altitudes or flight levels at 2,000-foot intervals.
Even cardinal altitudes or flight levels at 2,000-foot intervals.
Any cardinal altitude or flight level.
Odd cardinal flight levels at 4,000-foot intervals beginning with FL 450.

items.explanation: TBL 4-5-1 assigns even cardinal altitudes or flight levels at 2,000-foot intervals for courses 180 through 359 degrees at and below FL 410.

lookup_context: Aircraft is operating above FL 410
Magnetic course is 180 through 359 degrees
Use altitude assignment by flight direction
items.question_text: Above FL 410, what flight levels are assigned for courses 180 through 359 degrees magnetic?

choices.text: Odd cardinal flight levels at 4,000-foot intervals beginning with FL 430.
Even cardinal flight levels at 4,000-foot intervals beginning with FL 440.
Odd cardinal flight levels at 4,000-foot intervals beginning with FL 450.
Any altitude because direction-of-flight rules stop above FL 410.

items.explanation: Above FL 410, TBL 4-5-1 uses odd cardinal flight levels for both directions, but the 180-through-359-degree side begins with FL 430.

lookup_context: Prescribed minimum IFR altitude is at or above 18,000 feet MSL
Altimeter setting is 28.80 inches
Determine the adjusted minimum flight level
items.instruction: Use the minimum flight level adjustment table.
items.question_text: What adjustment factor applies for an altimeter setting of 28.80 inches?

choices.text: 500 feet.
1,000 feet.
1,500 feet.
2,000 feet.

items.explanation: TBL 4-5-3 assigns a 1,500-foot adjustment factor for altimeter settings from 28.91 through 28.42 inches.

lookup_context: Route segment ahead has a higher MEA
No MCA is specified at the fix
Aircraft must be climbed for the higher MEA
items.task: Choose when to start the climb.
items.question_text: If no MCA is specified, when must the aircraft be cleared to begin the climb to the higher MEA?

choices.text: Prior to or immediately after passing the fix where the higher MEA is designated.
Only after the pilot reports established on the next airway.
At least 10 miles before the fix.
Only after radar identification is established.

items.explanation: When a higher MEA applies and no MCA is specified, the aircraft must be cleared to begin the climb prior to or immediately after passing the fix where the higher MEA is designated.

lookup_context: Route segment ahead has a higher MEA
An MCA is specified at the fix
Aircraft must be at or above the MCA at the fix
items.question_text: If an MCA is specified, how must the climb clearance be handled?

choices.text: Issue the climb before the fix so the aircraft crosses at or above the MCA.
Wait until the aircraft has passed the fix, then climb to the new MEA.
Assign MOCA until the pilot requests the higher MEA.
Issue the climb only if the aircraft is GNSS equipped.

items.explanation: When an MCA is specified, the aircraft must be cleared to climb before the fix so it crosses the fix at or above the MCA.

lookup_context: Parallel runway thresholds are even
Aircraft departs one runway while another aircraft is on final to the other runway
Departure course immediately diverges at least 30 degrees from the missed approach course
items.question_text: With even parallel runway thresholds, what minimum runway centerline spacing is required?

choices.text: 1,000 feet.
2,500 feet.
3,600 feet.
8,300 feet.

items.explanation: For simultaneous departure/arrival operations with even parallel thresholds, runway centerlines must be at least 2,500 feet apart, along with the immediate 30-degree departure-course divergence condition.

lookup_context: Runways are nonintersecting
Runway edges do not touch
Departure/arrival operation uses diverging runways
items.question_text: For nonintersecting diverging runways, what minimum runway divergence is required?

choices.text: At least 15 degrees, with runway edges not touching.
At least 20 degrees, regardless of runway-edge geometry.
At least 30 degrees between runway centerlines.
No runway divergence is required if final approach courses are monitored.

items.explanation: Paragraph 5-8-5 allows the nonintersecting diverging-runway case when the runways diverge by 15 degrees or more and runway edges do not touch.

lookup_context: Simultaneous dependent approaches
Aircraft are during turn-on to adjacent final approach courses
Controller must maintain separation before final-course spacing applies
items.question_text: During turn-on, what minimum separation must be provided between aircraft?

choices.text: 1 mile diagonal radar separation.
1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar separation.
1.5 miles diagonal radar separation.
2 miles diagonal radar separation.

items.explanation: For simultaneous dependent approaches, provide at least 1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar separation between aircraft during turn-on.

lookup_context: Simultaneous dependent approaches
Runway centerlines are more than 8,300 feet but no more than 9,000 feet apart
Aircraft are successive on adjacent final approach courses
items.instruction: Use the simultaneous dependent approach spacing figure.
items.question_text: What minimum diagonal radar separation applies on adjacent final approach courses?

choices.text: 1 mile.
1.5 miles.
2 miles.
3 miles.

items.explanation: When runway centerlines are more than 8,300 feet but no more than 9,000 feet apart, the required diagonal radar separation between successive aircraft on adjacent finals is 2 miles.

lookup_context: Departing aircraft and arriving aircraft use the same airport
No appropriate fix supports the terminal 4-mile rule
Takeoff direction differs by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of final approach course
items.question_text: What minimum time before the arriving aircraft is estimated at the airport must the departing aircraft take off?

choices.text: 2 minutes.
3 minutes.
5 minutes.
10 minutes.

items.explanation: When takeoff direction differs by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of final approach course, the departure must take off 3 minutes before the arrival is estimated at the airport.

lookup_context: Departing aircraft and arriving aircraft use the same airport
Takeoff direction is other than the simple 45-degree case
Departure will establish on a course diverging by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of final approach course
items.question_text: What minimum timing applies before the departing aircraft must be established on the diverging course?

choices.text: Before the arrival reaches the FAF, regardless of time.
Five minutes before the arrival is estimated at the airport, or before it starts procedure turn.
Three minutes before the arrival is estimated at the airport.
After the arrival reports leaving the IAF outbound.

items.explanation: For the other takeoff-direction case, the departure must be established on a course diverging by at least 45 degrees 5 minutes before the arrival is estimated at the airport or before it starts procedure turn.

lookup_context: Aircraft are traveling opposite courses
Controller is using vertical separation until a discontinuation condition is met
Estimated passing time is known
items.question_text: How long must approved vertical separation be assigned around the estimated passing time?

choices.text: From 5 minutes before until 5 minutes after estimated passing.
From 10 minutes before until 10 minutes after estimated passing.
From 15 minutes before until 15 minutes after estimated passing.
Only until one aircraft reports level at its assigned altitude.

items.explanation: Opposite-course nonradar separation uses approved vertical separation from 10 minutes before until 10 minutes after the aircraft are estimated to pass, unless a listed discontinuation condition is met.

lookup_context: Two RNAV aircraft have reported passing the same position
Route segment is greater than 8 miles in width
No extended usable distance beyond 130 miles applies
items.task: Choose when vertical separation may be discontinued.
items.instruction: Apply the RNAV opposite-course discontinuation condition.
items.question_text: What minimum miles apart must the two RNAV aircraft be before vertical separation may be discontinued?

choices.text: 8 miles.
18 miles.
30 miles.
3 minutes apart, regardless of miles.

items.explanation: For two RNAV aircraft on a route segment greater than 8 miles wide, vertical separation may be discontinued after both pass the same position and are at least 18 miles apart, unless the extended-distance case requires 30 miles.

lookup_context: Route has been satisfactorily flight checked
Notice has been given to users
Route is below FL 240
items.instruction: Use the route protected-airspace reduction figure.
items.question_text: Below FL 240, what reduced protected-airspace profile may be used?

choices.text: 5 miles each side to 57.14 miles from the NAVAID, then expanding on a 5-degree angle.
10 miles each side to 57.14 miles from the NAVAID, then expanding on a 5-degree angle.
5 miles each side to 114.29 miles from the NAVAID, then expanding on a 10-degree angle.
10 miles each side to 114.29 miles from the NAVAID, then expanding on a 5-degree angle.

items.explanation: Below FL 240, the reduced route protected airspace is 5 miles on each side to 57.14 miles from the NAVAID, then expands on a 5-degree angle to the allowable lateral minimum.

lookup_context: Route has been satisfactorily flight checked
Notice has been given to users
Route is at or above FL 240
items.instruction: Use the route protected-airspace reduction figure.
items.question_text: At and above FL 240, what reduced protected-airspace profile may be used?

choices.text: 5 miles each side to 57.14 miles from the NAVAID, then expanding on a 5-degree angle.
10 miles each side to 114.29 miles from the NAVAID, then expanding on a 5-degree angle.
10 miles each side to 57.14 miles from the NAVAID, then expanding on a 10-degree angle.
20 miles each side to 114.29 miles from the NAVAID, then expanding on a 10-degree angle.

items.explanation: At and above FL 240, the reduced route protected airspace is 10 miles on each side to 114.29 miles from the NAVAID, then expands on a 5-degree angle.

lookup_context: Aircraft are crossing the offshore/oceanic airspace boundary below FL 180
Vertical separation is being applied
Standard altimeter setting and local altimeter setting may differ
items.instruction: Use the standard/local altimeter setting transition figure.
items.question_text: What must the controller ensure when applying vertical separation below FL 180 across the offshore/oceanic boundary?

choices.text: That QNE/QNH differences do not compromise separation.
That both aircraft are assigned the local altimeter setting before crossing.
That both aircraft are assigned standard altimeter setting at least 15 minutes before crossing.
That lateral separation replaces vertical separation before the boundary.

items.explanation: When vertical separation is applied below FL 180 across the offshore/oceanic boundary, control action must ensure standard-setting and local-setting differences do not compromise separation.

lookup_context: Aircraft is transitioning from offshore airspace to oceanic airspace
Aircraft will climb through opposite-direction oceanic traffic
Controller plans to use the offshore/oceanic transition procedure
items.question_text: When must vertical separation above the opposite-direction traffic be established?

choices.text: Before crossing the boundary and 15 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.
After crossing the boundary but before radar service is terminated.
Five minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.
Only before the outbound aircraft reaches oceanic cruising altitude.

items.explanation: For opposite-direction offshore-to-oceanic transition, vertical separation above the traffic must be established before the outbound crosses the offshore/oceanic boundary and 15 minutes before estimated passing.

lookup_context: Temporary stationary airspace reservation
Controller chooses lateral separation
Aircraft will be cleared around the reservation
items.instruction: Use the temporary stationary reservation lateral figure.
items.question_text: How must the route be cleared laterally around the temporary stationary reservation?

choices.text: So protected airspace along the route does not overlap the reservation area.
So the route centerline remains outside the reservation area.
So the aircraft is at least 15 minutes from the reservation boundary.
So only the aircraft's actual track avoids the reservation boundary.

items.explanation: For lateral separation from a temporary stationary reservation, clear the aircraft so protected airspace along the route of flight does not overlap the geographical area of the reservation.

lookup_context: Temporary stationary airspace reservation
Controller chooses vertical separation
Aircraft may be laterally within a protected area around the reservation
items.instruction: Use the temporary stationary reservation vertical figure.
items.question_text: Within what area must vertical separation exist?

choices.text: Only inside the published reservation boundary.
Inside the reservation plus a perimeter buffer equal to one-half the lateral separation minimum.
Inside the reservation plus a fixed 5-mile buffer.
Inside the reservation plus a 15-minute longitudinal buffer.

items.explanation: For vertical separation from a temporary stationary reservation, vertical separation must exist while the aircraft is within the reservation plus a perimeter buffer equivalent to one-half the lateral separation minimum.
